faq.a: Yes. Powered USB hubs with buggy firmware or incompatible drivers are a known trigger. Try connecting devices directly to the computer.

faq.q: Is this specific to Windows 10/11?
faq.a: WDF is present in Windows 8.1 and later, so this BSOD can occur on those versions. It does not affect Windows 7 systems using older WDM drivers.

title: Resource Not Owned
shortDescription: A thread attempted to release a resource it did not own.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a kernel thread tries to release a resource (like a lock or mutex) that it does not currently own. This is a programming error in a driver or kernel component that indicates improper synchronization handling.
symptoms: Blue screen with RESOURCE_NOT_OWNED
Random crashes during file operations
System hangs before BSOD
Crash occurs during multi-threaded workloads

fixSteps.title: Analyze crash dump
fixSteps.instruction: Use BlueScreenView to identify the faulting driver from minidump files.

fixSteps.title: Update all drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all device drivers, focusing on storage and filesystem filter drivers.

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Test RAM with Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86.

fixSteps.title: Remove third-party filter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall antivirus, backup, and encryption software that install kernel filter drivers.

whenToCallPro: If crash dump analysis does not reveal the faulting driver, professional kernel debugging is needed.

faq.q: What is a kernel resource?
faq.a: A kernel resource is a synchronization mechanism (like an executive resource or mutex) used to control access to shared data structures in the kernel.

faq.q: Is this a common BSOD?
faq.a: No. It is relatively uncommon and usually indicates a specific driver bug rather than a general system problem.

faq.q: Can this be caused by antivirus software?
faq.a: Yes. Antivirus and backup software that use filesystem filter drivers are known to have synchronization bugs that can trigger this error.

title: Multiple IRP Complete Requests
shortDescription: A driver attempted to complete an I/O Request Packet (IRP) that was already completed.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a driver calls IoCompleteRequest on an IRP (I/O Request Packet) that has already been completed. This is always a driver bug - the driver is completing the same I/O operation twice, which corrupts the I/O manager's internal structures.

causes.cause: Driver bug completing I/O requests twice
Race condition in driver I/O handling

symptoms: Blue screen with MULTIPLE_IRP_COMPLETE_REQUESTS
Crash during heavy disk I/O
BSOD triggered by specific file operations
System instability with storage-intensive tasks

fixSteps.title: Identify the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Use BlueScreenView to analyze crash dumps and identify the driver responsible.

fixSteps.title: Update the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Download and install the latest version of the identified driver from the manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow

fixSteps.title: Run Driver Verifier
fixSteps.instruction: From elevated Command Prompt: verifier /standard /all. This will catch the double-completion bug. Run verifier /reset when done.

whenToCallPro: If Driver Verifier catches the bug but no updated driver is available, the driver vendor needs to fix the bug.

faq.q: What is an IRP?
faq.a: An I/O Request Packet is a kernel data structure used to communicate I/O requests between the I/O manager and device drivers. Each IRP should be completed exactly once.

faq.q: Can this cause data corruption?
faq.a: Yes. Completing an IRP twice can corrupt the I/O manager's structures and potentially lead to data corruption on disk. The BSOD prevents this.

faq.q: Is Driver Verifier safe to use?
faq.a: Driver Verifier stress-tests drivers and may cause additional BSODs if drivers have bugs. Use it for diagnosis only and disable it (verifier /reset) after identifying the faulting driver.

title: IRQL Not at Dispatch Level
shortDescription: A kernel operation that requires DISPATCH_LEVEL IRQL was performed at an incorrect IRQL.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a kernel operation that must run at DISPATCH_LEVEL IRQL is executed at a different IRQL. This is always a driver or kernel component programming error. It is relatively rare compared to IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL.

causes.cause: Driver bug with incorrect IRQL management

symptoms: Blue screen with IRQL_NOT_DISPATCH_LEVEL
Random system crashes
Crash during specific hardware operations
BSOD after driver update

fixSteps.title: Identify and update the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Check minidump files with BlueScreenView and update the identified driver.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Remove recently installed software
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall software installed shortly before the crashes began.

fixSteps.title: System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: Use System Restore to revert to a state before the crashes started.

whenToCallPro: If the faulting driver cannot be identified through minidump analysis.

faq.q: What is DISPATCH_LEVEL?
faq.a: DISPATCH_LEVEL is an interrupt request level in Windows where thread scheduling occurs. Certain kernel operations can only be performed at this level.

faq.q: How is this different from IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL?
faq.a: IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL occurs when IRQL is too high to access paged memory. IRQL_NOT_DISPATCH_LEVEL is about an operation requiring a specific IRQL that was not met.

faq.q: Is this a rare error?
faq.a: Yes, it is less common than most IRQL-related BSODs. It typically affects specific driver versions with IRQL management bugs.

title: Hardware Data Bus Error
shortDescription: A parity error on the system data bus was detected, indicating a hardware memory fault.
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates a hardware parity error was detected on the system memory bus. This is almost always caused by faulty RAM, though it can also be caused by motherboard bus failures. The error means data was corrupted during transfer between the CPU and RAM.
symptoms: Blue screen with DATA_BUS_ERROR
System crashes randomly, especially under memory load
Error occurs after adding new RAM
Memory-related errors in Event Viewer

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Run MemTest86 from a bootable USB for at least 8 passes to thoroughly test RAM.

fixSteps.title: Reseat RAM modules
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, open case, remove all RAM sticks, clean contacts with a dry cloth, and reseat firmly.

fixSteps.title: Test individual RAM sticks
fixSteps.instruction: Install one RAM stick at a time and test. Identify which module causes the error.

fixSteps.title: Check RAM compatibility
fixSteps.instruction: Verify your RAM is on the motherboard's QVL (Qualified Vendor List). Check speeds and timings match the motherboard specifications.

whenToCallPro: If all RAM sticks individually test fine but the error persists, the motherboard memory controller or bus may be failing.

faq.q: What is a parity error?
faq.a: A parity error means data was corrupted during transfer on the memory bus. The system detected that the data read from RAM does not match what was written.

faq.q: Can I fix faulty RAM?
faq.a: No. Faulty RAM modules must be replaced. Check if your RAM is still under warranty for a free replacement.

faq.q: Does mixing RAM brands cause this?
faq.a: It can. Mixing RAM with different speeds, timings, or voltages can cause bus errors. For best stability, use identical modules.

title: No More IRP Stack Locations
shortDescription: An I/O request was sent to a driver with no remaining IRP stack locations available.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a higher-level driver sends an I/O request down the driver stack but there are no more stack locations available for the next lower driver. This typically happens when there are too many filter drivers layered on top of each other.

causes.cause: Too many filesystem filter drivers (antivirus, backup, encryption)
Driver miscalculating required IRP stack size

symptoms: Blue screen with NO_MORE_IRP_STACK_LOCATIONS
Crash when multiple security products are active
BSOD during file operations with encrypted volumes
System becomes unstable after installing additional security software

fixSteps.title: Remove extra security software
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall redundant antivirus, anti-malware, and endpoint protection products. Keep only one antivirus solution.

fixSteps.title: Remove unnecessary filter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall backup agents, disk encryption, and storage management software that install filter drivers.

fixSteps.title: Increase IRP stack size in registry
fixSteps.instruction: Open Registry Editor. Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters. Create or modify IRPStackSize DWORD and set to 18-50.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after removing filter drivers, professional driver stack analysis is needed.

faq.q: What is an IRP stack?
faq.a: Each I/O request in Windows has a stack of entries, one for each driver it passes through. When a system has many layered filter drivers, this stack can run out of space.

faq.q: Can running two antivirus programs cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Multiple antivirus products each add filesystem filter drivers. This is a well-known cause of IRP stack exhaustion.

faq.q: What is a safe IRPStackSize value?
faq.a: The default is 15. Values between 18 and 50 are generally safe. Do not set it above 50 as excessive values waste kernel memory.

title: Thread Stuck in Device Driver
shortDescription: A thread in a device driver is stuck in an infinite loop, commonly the display driver.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a device driver thread enters an infinite loop, most commonly the display driver. The system detects that the thread has been spinning for too long without yielding. This is closely related to VIDEO_TDR_FAILURE but can affect non-display drivers as well.

causes.cause: Display driver infinite loop or hang
Driver bug triggered by specific workload

symptoms: Blue screen with THREAD_STUCK_IN_DEVICE_DRIVER
Screen freezes completely before BSOD
Display artifacts appear before crash
System hangs during graphics-intensive operations

fixSteps.title: Clean install GPU drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Boot into Safe Mode, run DDU to remove all GPU drivers, reboot, and install the latest stable version.

fixSteps.title: Check GPU temperatures
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor GPU temperature with MSI Afterburner. Clean GPU fans and heatsink if temperatures are high.

fixSteps.title: Remove GPU overclock
fixSteps.instruction: Reset GPU core and memory clocks to default values.

fixSteps.title: Test GPU stability
fixSteps.instruction: Run FurMark or 3DMark to stress test the GPU and watch for crashes or artifacts.

whenToCallPro: If the GPU fails stress tests at stock clocks with good cooling, the GPU hardware may be failing.

faq.q: Is this always a GPU problem?
faq.a: Usually, but not always. While the display driver is the most common culprit, any device driver can get stuck in a loop.

faq.q: Can this damage my GPU?
faq.a: The BSOD itself does not damage hardware. However, if overheating is the cause, continued operation at high temperatures can degrade the GPU over time.

faq.q: Should I try an older GPU driver?
faq.a: If the latest driver causes issues, trying the previous stable version is a valid troubleshooting step. Some driver versions have known bugs with specific GPU models.

title: Special Pool Detected Memory Corruption
shortDescription: Driver Verifier detected that a driver corrupted memory in the special pool.
fullDescription: This BSOD is generated by Driver Verifier's special pool feature when it detects that a driver has written beyond the bounds of its allocated memory. This error only occurs when Driver Verifier is enabled and means a specific driver has a memory corruption bug.

causes.cause: Driver writing beyond allocated buffer (detected by Verifier)
Outdated driver with known buffer overflow
Driver incompatible with current Windows version

symptoms: Blue screen with SPECIAL_POOL_DETECTED_MEMORY_CORRUPTION while Driver Verifier is running
Crash identifies specific driver file
Error occurs only when Driver Verifier is enabled
System is stable without Driver Verifier

fixSteps.title: Note the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: The BSOD message identifies exactly which driver corrupted memory. Record this information.

fixSteps.title: Update or remove the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Download the latest version of the identified driver. If no update is available, consider alternative hardware or drivers.

fixSteps.title: Disable Driver Verifier
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: verifier /reset. Restart the computer.

fixSteps.title: Report the bug to driver vendor
fixSteps.instruction: Contact the hardware manufacturer and report the Driver Verifier finding with the crash dump.

whenToCallPro: If the faulting driver is essential and no updated version is available, professional driver debugging or alternative solutions may be needed.

faq.q: Should I keep Driver Verifier running?
faq.a: No. Driver Verifier is a diagnostic tool that adds overhead and can cause additional BSODs. Disable it after identifying the problematic driver.

faq.q: Will this crash happen without Driver Verifier?
faq.a: The driver bug exists regardless, but without Verifier it may not crash immediately. Instead, it silently corrupts memory, which can lead to other BSODs later.

faq.q: Can I ignore this if my system works without Verifier?
faq.a: The driver still has a bug that corrupts memory. While it may not crash often, it can cause random instability and data corruption. Updating the driver is recommended.

title: System Out of Memory
shortDescription: The system ran out of memory (both physical RAM and virtual memory/page file).
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when Windows completely exhausts all available memory, including physical RAM and the page file. The system can no longer allocate memory for critical operations and halts. This can be caused by memory leaks in applications or drivers, insufficient RAM for the workload, or a page file that is too small or disabled.

causes.cause: Page file disabled or too small

symptoms: Blue screen with STATUS_NO_MEMORY
System becomes extremely slow before crash
Applications fail to launch or crash with out-of-memory errors
Task Manager shows high memory usage
Disk activity at 100% from paging

fixSteps.title: Enable and size page file properly
fixSteps.instruction: Go to System Properties > Advanced > Performance Settings > Advanced > Virtual Memory. Set page file to System managed or 1.5x your RAM size.

fixSteps.title: Identify memory-leaking applications
fixSteps.instruction: Open Task Manager > Details tab > right-click column headers > add Commit Size. Sort by Commit Size to find memory hogs.

fixSteps.title: Update drivers to fix leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Update all drivers, especially GPU, network, and storage drivers which are common sources of kernel memory leaks.

fixSteps.title: Add more RAM
fixSteps.instruction: If your system consistently uses over 80% of RAM during normal use, consider adding more physical memory.

whenToCallPro: If memory leaks are identified but the source driver or application cannot be determined, professional performance analysis may be needed.

faq.q: How much RAM do I need?
faq.a: For general use, 8GB is minimum. For gaming and multitasking, 16GB is recommended. For professional workloads, 32GB or more may be needed.

faq.q: Should I disable the page file?
faq.a: No. Disabling the page file can cause this BSOD. Even with ample RAM, Windows uses the page file for stability. Keep it enabled.

faq.q: What is a memory leak?
faq.a: A memory leak occurs when software allocates memory but fails to release it when done. Over time, this consumes all available memory until the system runs out.
